techniques.name: Few-Shot Prompting
techniques.when_to_use: When you need the LLM to follow a specific input/output format or coding pattern
techniques.description: Provide 2-3 concrete examples of desired input-output pairs before the actual task
techniques.evidence: Brown et al. (2020); Rachitsky (2025) reports few-shot can raise accuracy from near 0% to 90% on coding tasks — the most robustly supported technique for code generation
techniques.example:
Example 1: Input: [1,3,2] -> Output: [1,2,3]
Example 2: Input: [5,1] -> Output: [1,5]
Now sort: [4,2,7]

techniques.name: Specification Pattern
techniques.when_to_use: When the prompt lacks concrete requirements like types, constraints, or edge cases
techniques.description: Explicitly state input types, output format, constraints, error handling, and edge cases
techniques.evidence: White et al. (2023) — Vanderbilt Prompt Pattern Catalog; structured specifications reduce ambiguity in LLM outputs
techniques.example: Write a function that: takes a list of integers, returns the sorted list in ascending order, handles empty lists by returning [], raises TypeError for non-integer elements.

techniques.name: Output Format Specification
techniques.when_to_use: When the desired output structure is ambiguous or could be interpreted multiple ways
techniques.description: Explicitly define the expected output format, including code structure, return types, and documentation
techniques.evidence: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Guide (2024) — explicit format instructions significantly reduce output variance
techniques.example: Return only the Python function with type hints. Include a docstring. Do not include import statements or example usage.

techniques.name: Constraint Enumeration
techniques.when_to_use: When there are non-obvious requirements like performance, memory, or compatibility constraints
techniques.description: List all constraints as explicit numbered requirements the solution must satisfy
techniques.evidence: White et al. (2023) — Prompt Pattern Catalog; enumerated constraints reduce missed requirements in generated code
techniques.example: Requirements: 1. Time complexity must be O(n log n). 2. Must work with Python 3.10+. 3. No external dependencies.

techniques.name: Template Pattern
techniques.when_to_use: When you want the LLM output to follow a specific structure or skeleton
techniques.description: Provide a template with placeholders that the model should fill in
techniques.evidence: White et al. (2023) — Prompt Pattern Catalog; templates reduce structural deviation in outputs
techniques.example:
Fill in this template:
```python
def FUNC_NAME(PARAMS) -> RETURN_TYPE:
    """DOCSTRING"""
    IMPLEMENTATION
```

techniques.name: Context Grounding
techniques.when_to_use: When the task requires understanding of existing code, APIs, or system architecture
techniques.description: Include relevant context like existing code, API signatures, or system design before stating the task
techniques.evidence: OpenAI Prompt Engineering Guide (2024) — providing reference context reduces hallucination in code generation
techniques.example:
Given this existing User class:
```python
class User:
    def __init__(self, name: str, email: str): ...
```
Add a method to validate the email format.

techniques.name: Negative Prompting
techniques.when_to_use: When there are common mistakes or antipatterns you want to explicitly avoid
techniques.description: State what the model should NOT do, in addition to what it should do
techniques.evidence: Schulhoff et al. (2024) — The Prompt Report; negative constraints reduce common error patterns in generated code
techniques.example: Do NOT use global variables. Do NOT catch generic Exception. Do NOT use mutable default arguments.

techniques.name: Decomposition Prompting
techniques.when_to_use: Complex tasks that involve multiple distinct sub-problems
techniques.description: Break the task into numbered sub-tasks and ask the model to solve each sequentially
techniques.evidence: Khot et al. (2023) — decomposed prompting outperforms chain-of-thought on complex compositional tasks
techniques.example: Break this into steps: 1. Parse the CSV file. 2. Validate each row. 3. Transform valid rows. 4. Write output JSON.

techniques.name: Test-Driven Prompting
techniques.when_to_use: When correctness is critical and you can provide test cases upfront
techniques.description: Include expected test cases or assertions that the generated code must satisfy
techniques.evidence: Chen et al. (2023) — including test cases in prompts improves pass@1 on HumanEval by 12-15%
techniques.example:
The function must pass these tests:
assert sort_list([3,1,2]) == [1,2,3]
assert sort_list([]) == []
assert sort_list([1]) == [1]

techniques.name: Cognitive Verifier Pattern
techniques.when_to_use: When the prompt is vague and would benefit from the LLM asking clarifying questions first
techniques.description: Instruct the model to generate clarifying questions about the task before solving it
techniques.evidence: White et al. (2023) — Prompt Pattern Catalog; cognitive verification reduces assumption errors
techniques.example: Before writing code, list 3 questions you would ask to clarify this requirement. Then answer them with reasonable assumptions and proceed.

techniques.name: Language and Framework Specification
techniques.when_to_use: When the target language, version, or framework is not specified in the prompt
techniques.description: Explicitly state the programming language, version, and any frameworks or libraries to use
techniques.evidence: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Guide (2024) — language specification prevents cross-language confusion in code generation
techniques.example: Write this in Python 3.12 using only the standard library. Use type hints and follow PEP 8.

techniques.name: Error Handling Specification
techniques.when_to_use: When the prompt doesn't mention how errors or edge cases should be handled
techniques.description: Explicitly describe expected error handling behavior, including what exceptions to raise or catch
techniques.evidence: Schulhoff et al. (2024) — The Prompt Report; prompts lacking error handling specs produce inconsistent error behavior
techniques.example: If the file does not exist, raise FileNotFoundError with a descriptive message. If the JSON is malformed, return None.

techniques.name: Structured XML/Markdown Prompting
techniques.when_to_use: Complex prompts with multiple sections that need clear delineation
techniques.description: Use XML tags or markdown headers to clearly separate different parts of the prompt
techniques.evidence: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Guide (2024) — XML-structured prompts improve Claude's ability to follow multi-part instructions
techniques.example:
<task>Sort a list</task>
<constraints>O(n log n) time</constraints>
<output_format>Python function with type hints</output_format>

techniques.name: Minimal Context Principle
techniques.when_to_use: When the prompt contains irrelevant information that could confuse the model
techniques.description: Include only the context directly relevant to the task; remove unrelated background
techniques.evidence: Shi et al. (2023) — irrelevant context in prompts degrades LLM performance, especially on reasoning tasks
techniques.example: Instead of pasting an entire file, extract only the relevant function signature and types needed for the task.

antipatterns.name: Role Prompting for Code Quality
antipatterns.description: Assigning a persona like 'act as a senior engineer' or 'you are an expert Python developer' to improve code output quality
antipatterns.why_ineffective: Role/persona assignment may shape tone and formatting but does not meaningfully alter the logical structure or quality of generated code
antipatterns.evidence: Schulhoff et al. (2025) find role prompting has 'little to no effect on improving correctness'; widely adopted due to intuition rather than empirical validation
antipatterns.misconception: Developers assume that telling the model to 'be an expert' will produce expert-level code. The model's capabilities are fixed regardless of assigned persona.
antipatterns.instead: Use concrete techniques: provide examples (few-shot), specify constraints explicitly, include test cases. These directly improve output quality where role prompting does not.

antipatterns.name: Politeness/Emotional Appeals
antipatterns.description: Using phrases like 'please', 'this is very important', 'my job depends on this', or 'take a deep breath' to improve output quality
antipatterns.why_ineffective: Emotional framing and politeness markers do not affect the model's reasoning capabilities or code generation quality
antipatterns.evidence: Schulhoff et al. (2024) — The Prompt Report; no empirical evidence that emotional appeals improve task performance on coding benchmarks
antipatterns.misconception: Anthropomorphizing the model leads to the belief that motivation or pressure improves its output. LLMs are not motivated by social cues.
antipatterns.instead: Replace emotional appeals with specific, actionable requirements. 'This is critical, please be careful' adds no information; 'handle null inputs by returning an empty list' does.

antipatterns.name: Vague Quality Adjectives
antipatterns.description: Using words like 'good', 'clean', 'best', 'production-ready', or 'high-quality' without defining what those mean
antipatterns.why_ineffective: These terms are subjective and provide no concrete criteria for the model to optimize against
antipatterns.evidence: Anthropic Prompt Engineering Guide (2024) — vague qualitative descriptors produce inconsistent outputs; concrete criteria outperform subjective instructions
antipatterns.misconception: Developers assume the model shares their mental model of 'clean code' or 'production-ready'. Different developers mean different things by these terms.
antipatterns.instead: Define quality concretely: 'include type hints, handle errors with specific exceptions, keep functions under 20 lines, add docstrings for public functions.'

antipatterns.name: Chain-of-Thought for Simple Code Tasks
antipatterns.description: Asking the model to 'think step by step' for straightforward code generation that doesn't involve complex reasoning
antipatterns.why_ineffective: CoT improves multi-step reasoning but adds unnecessary verbosity for simple generation tasks, and modern models already perform internal reasoning
antipatterns.evidence: Wei et al. (2022) show CoT benefits complex reasoning; Schulhoff et al. (2025) note the effect fades for straightforward generation. Modern coding assistants incorporate CoT-style reasoning internally.
antipatterns.misconception: If step-by-step helps for math, it must help for all tasks. In practice, for simple code generation it just adds latency without improving correctness.
antipatterns.instead: Reserve CoT for genuinely complex tasks (algorithm design, multi-step transformations). For simple functions, a clear specification is more effective.

antipatterns.name: Excessive Prompt Length
antipatterns.description: Including large amounts of tangential context, background, or multiple unrelated instructions in a single prompt
antipatterns.why_ineffective: Irrelevant context actively degrades LLM performance. The model may attend to irrelevant details instead of the core task.
antipatterns.evidence: Shi et al. (2023) — demonstrated that adding irrelevant information to prompts measurably degrades performance on reasoning tasks
antipatterns.misconception: More context is always better. In reality, signal-to-noise ratio matters — excessive context dilutes the actual requirements.
antipatterns.instead: Apply the Minimal Context Principle: include only what's directly relevant to the task. If the model needs background, provide it in a structured, clearly delineated section.
